journal_entry.description: Purchase of delivery vehicle with mixed payment: 50% cash and 50% promissory note
journal_entry.rationale: The delivery vehicle is a capital asset acquired for $40,000. Under IFRS, the vehicle should be capitalized at its purchase price. The debit tuple (1,0,0,0,0,0) requires one asset increase line for the vehicle. The credit tuple (1,0,0,1,0,0) requires one liability increase line (the promissory note for $20,000) and one asset decrease line (cash payment of $20,000). Total debits = $40,000 (vehicle). Total credits = $20,000 (cash) + $20,000 (notes payable) = $40,000. The entry balances and properly reflects the mixed financing arrangement.

output.reason: The delivery vehicle is an asset being acquired. On the debit side, there is 1 asset increase (the vehicle itself). The payment method (50% cash + 50% promissory note) affects only the credit side - the debit side records only the single asset acquisition.

debit_classifier.reason: The delivery vehicle is an asset being acquired. On the debit side, there is 1 asset increase (the vehicle itself). The payment method (50% cash + 50% promissory note) affects only the credit side - the debit side records only the single asset acquisition.

output.reason: Atlas Van Lines purchases a delivery vehicle (asset) for $40,000. Payment is split: $20,000 cash (50%) and $20,000 promissory note (50%). On the credit side: (1) Cash payment = asset decrease (cash leaving), and (2) Promissory note issued = liability increase (creating an obligation to pay). This results in one asset decrease line and one liability increase line.

credit_classifier.reason: Atlas Van Lines purchases a delivery vehicle (asset) for $40,000. Payment is split: $20,000 cash (50%) and $20,000 promissory note (50%). On the credit side: (1) Cash payment = asset decrease (cash leaving), and (2) Promissory note issued = liability increase (creating an obligation to pay). This results in one asset decrease line and one liability increase line.
